privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: Neetpix is committed to protecting your privacy. Most of our tools process your files entirely in your browser — your data never touches our servers.
privacy.localProcessingDesc: The following tools process all data entirely in your browser. Files you upload are never sent to any server:
privacy.localProcessingTools: Remove Background, Image to PDF, PDF to Word, Image Compress
privacy.apiProcessingDesc: The Screenshot Translate tool runs OCR locally in your browser, but sends the recognized text to our translation API proxy for translation. The text is not stored on our servers and is discarded immediately after translation.
privacy.dataRetentionDesc: We do not store your files or translated text. All processing is ephemeral. We only collect anonymous usage analytics (page views, tool usage counts) via Vercel Analytics, which does not use cookies or track individual users.
privacy.thirdPartyDesc: We use the following third-party services: Vercel (hosting and analytics), MyMemory Translation API (for screenshot translation). These services have their own privacy policies.
privacy.contactDesc: If you have questions about this privacy policy, please contact us at privacy@neetpix.com

about.title: About Neetpix
about.description: Neetpix is a free online tool platform built on a privacy-first principle. All processing happens in your browser - your files never leave your device.
about.intro: Neetpix was born from a simple frustration: most online tool sites are bloated with ads, require signups, upload your files to unknown servers, or lock core features behind paywalls. We wanted to build something different - a clean, fast, and genuinely free tool platform that respects your privacy.
about.privacyDesc: Every tool on Neetpix processes your files directly in your browser using WebAssembly and modern web APIs. Your files are never uploaded to any server. This isn't just a marketing claim - it's a technical architecture decision.
about.toolsPdf: 12 PDF tools - convert, merge, split, compress, watermark, encrypt, crop, rotate and more
about.toolsImage: 10 image tools - format convert, EXIF strip, resize/crop, grid split, ID photo, OCR, blur, remove background, compress, watermark
about.toolsGenerator: 3 generator tools - QR code generator, QR decoder, and chart generator, fully local
about.toolsTranslate: 1 translation tool - screenshot OCR translation with auto-detect
about.toolsNetwork: 1 network tool - direct file transfer between devices via WebRTC, no server relay
about.toolsTotal: 27 tools in total — all free, no signup, no upload

faq.title: FAQ
faq.removeBackground1a: Yes, completely free. AI runs in your browser, so we have no server costs.
faq.removeBackground2a: JPG, PNG, and WebP up to 20MB.
faq.removeBackground3: Does the free version have a watermark?
faq.removeBackground3a: Yes, the free version includes a small Neetpix watermark. Future Pro version will offer watermark-free downloads.
faq.imageToPdf1: Can I reorder images after uploading?
faq.imageToPdf1a: Yes, drag and drop any thumbnail to reorder.
faq.imageToPdf2a: A4, Letter, or fit to original image size. Portrait or landscape orientation.
faq.imageToPdf3: Is there a limit on the number of images?
faq.imageToPdf3a: No hard limit, but we recommend under 50 images for best performance.
faq.pdfToWord1: Does PDF to Word preserve formatting?
faq.pdfToWord1a: This tool extracts text content. Complex layouts, images, and tables may not be fully preserved.
faq.pdfToWord2: Is there a file size limit?
faq.pdfToWord2a: No hard limit. Processing happens in your browser, so very large files may fail with insufficient memory — try splitting the PDF first.
faq.pdfToWord3a: Scanned PDFs without a text layer cannot be extracted. The tool requires selectable text in the PDF.
faq.wordToPdf1: Is the Word to PDF converter free?
faq.wordToPdf1a: Yes, completely free with no limits on file count or size (up to 50MB per file). No signup, no watermark, no hidden fees.
faq.wordToPdf2: Are my documents uploaded to a server?
faq.wordToPdf2a: No. All processing happens in your browser. Your documents never leave your device, ensuring complete privacy.
faq.wordToPdf3a: We preserve text content with basic formatting including headings, paragraphs, and lists. Complex layouts, images, and tables may be simplified.
faq.screenshotTranslate1a: English and Chinese, in both directions (EN→ZH and ZH→EN), plus auto-detect.
faq.screenshotTranslate2: Can I paste a screenshot directly?
faq.screenshotTranslate2a: Yes, press Ctrl+V (or Cmd+V on Mac) to paste a screenshot from your clipboard.
faq.screenshotTranslate3a: OCR accuracy depends on image clarity. Translation uses the MyMemory API. For best results, use clear, high-contrast screenshots.
faq.imageCompress1a: You control the quality level (1-100%). Lower quality means smaller file size but more visible compression artifacts.
faq.imageCompress2: Can I convert formats during compression?
faq.imageCompress2a: Yes, you can output as JPG, PNG, or WebP regardless of the original format.
faq.imageCompress3: Is there a batch download option?
faq.imageCompress3a: Yes, upload multiple images and click Download All to get a ZIP file.
faq.pdfMerge1a: Yes, completely free. All merging happens in your browser with no server costs.
faq.pdfMerge2: Can I reorder PDF files before merging?
faq.pdfMerge2a: Yes, use the up/down arrows next to each file to change the order.
faq.pdfMerge3: How many PDFs can I merge?
faq.pdfMerge3a: There's no hard limit, but we recommend under 20 files for best performance.
faq.pdfSplit1: What page range formats are supported?
faq.pdfSplit1a: Use formats like 1-3,5,7-9. Single pages and ranges separated by commas are both supported.
faq.pdfSplit2: Can I split into multiple files at once?
faq.pdfSplit2a: Yes, enter multiple ranges separated by commas. Each range generates a separate PDF file.
faq.pdfSplit3a: Password-protected PDFs cannot be split. Please remove the password first.
faq.pdfCompress1a: This tool uses lossless structural compression. It re-serializes the PDF to reduce size without degrading content.
faq.pdfCompress2: How much can I reduce the file size?
faq.pdfCompress2a: Results vary. Already-optimized PDFs may see little reduction, while verbose PDFs can shrink significantly.
faq.pdfCompress3: What's the difference between Light and Strong mode?
faq.pdfCompress3a: Light mode uses lossless structural compression (best for text PDFs, 5-20% smaller). Strong mode rasterizes each page as a compressed JPEG image (best for scanned/image PDFs, up to 80% smaller, but text becomes non-searchable).
faq.pdfWatermark1: Can I customize the watermark text?
faq.pdfWatermark1a: Yes, enter any text. The default is CONFIDENTIAL.
faq.pdfWatermark2a: Center places one watermark in the middle, while Tiled repeats it across the whole page.
faq.pdfWatermark3a: Yes. We render watermark text via Canvas, which supports Chinese, English, and any language your browser supports.
faq.pdfPageNumbers1: Can I choose where page numbers appear?
faq.pdfPageNumbers1a: Yes, choose bottom center, bottom left, or bottom right.
faq.pdfPageNumbers2: Can I start from a different number?
faq.pdfPageNumbers2a: Yes, set any start number. The first page will use that number.
faq.pdfPageNumbers3a: Plain number (1), Page X, or Page X / Y. All use the Helvetica font.
faq.imageWatermark1a: Completely free. All processing happens in your browser, no server costs.
faq.imageWatermark2: Does it support Chinese watermark text?
faq.imageWatermark2a: Yes. We use a system font stack including PingFang SC and Microsoft YaHei, so Chinese renders cleanly without garbled characters.
faq.imageWatermark3: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageWatermark3a: No. All images are processed locally in your browser and never leave your device—ideal for sensitive documents like ID cards.
faq.pdfEncrypt1a: Yes. We use AES-256 encryption, the industry standard for PDF protection. All processing happens in your browser.
faq.pdfEncrypt2: Can I recover a forgotten password?
faq.pdfEncrypt2a: No. PDF encryption is one-way. If you forget the password, the PDF cannot be recovered. Please store your password safely.
faq.pdfEncrypt3: Is there a file size limit?
faq.pdfEncrypt3a: Yes, 50MB per file. Processing happens in your browser, so very large files may be slow.
faq.pdfDecrypt1: Can I decrypt a PDF without knowing the password?
faq.pdfDecrypt1a: No. You must know the password to remove it. This tool is for removing passwords from PDFs you own, not for cracking.
faq.pdfDecrypt2a: Yes, as long as you own the PDF or have permission. This tool is for removing passwords from your own documents.
faq.pdfDecrypt3: What if I enter the wrong password?
faq.pdfDecrypt3a: You'll see an error message. Simply re-enter the correct password and try again.
faq.pdfCrop1a: You enter a percentage (0-100) for each side — top, right, bottom, left. That portion of the page is trimmed from that edge. Top+Bottom and Left+Right must each total less than 100%.
faq.pdfCrop2: Will cropping reduce the file size?
faq.pdfCrop2a: Usually not significantly. Cropping only changes the visible page area; the underlying content stream is preserved, so file size stays roughly the same.
faq.pdfCrop3a: No. Password-protected PDFs cannot be cropped. Please remove the password first using the PDF Decrypt tool.
faq.pdfRotate1: Can I rotate only specific pages?
faq.pdfRotate1a: Yes. Choose "Specific pages" and enter page ranges like 1-3,5,7-9. The other pages keep their original orientation.
faq.pdfRotate2a: 90° (clockwise), 180°, and 270° (counter-clockwise). Pick the angle that fits your document.
faq.pdfRotate3a: No. Rotation only updates the page's orientation metadata, so the content quality is fully preserved.
faq.imageConvert1: Is HEIC to JPG conversion really free?
faq.imageConvert1a: Yes, completely free with no limits. All conversion happens in your browser, so we have no server costs and your photos stay private.
faq.imageConvert2: Can I convert multiple images at once?
faq.imageConvert2a: Yes, upload multiple images and we'll convert them all in one go. Download each file individually or grab them as a ZIP.
faq.imageConvert3a: JPG, PNG, WebP, AVIF, and HEIC. Convert in any direction — for example, HEIC from an iPhone to JPG for broader compatibility.
faq.imageExif1: What is EXIF metadata and why should I care?
faq.imageExif1a: EXIF data includes GPS location, camera info, and timestamps embedded by your camera or phone. Stripping it protects your privacy before sharing photos online.
faq.imageExif2: Does stripping EXIF affect image quality?
faq.imageExif2a: No, stripping only removes metadata. The image pixels remain identical, so quality and resolution are fully preserved.
faq.imageExif3: Are my photos uploaded to a server?
faq.imageExif3a: No. All EXIF processing happens locally in your browser, so your photos never leave your device — ideal for protecting GPS data in personal photos.
faq.imageResize1: What social media presets are available?
faq.imageResize1a: Instagram Square (1:1), Instagram Story (9:16), Twitter Post (16:9), and more. Pick a preset and we'll resize to the exact dimensions automatically.
faq.imageResize2: Can I resize multiple images at once?
faq.imageResize2a: Yes, resize mode supports batch processing. Upload multiple images, apply the same dimensions, and download them all as a ZIP file.
faq.imageResize3a: Resizing changes pixel dimensions, which may soften detail when upscaling. We use high-quality Lanczos resampling to minimize loss, and downscaling preserves detail well.
faq.imageIdPhoto1: Is the ID photo generation really free?
faq.imageIdPhoto1a: Yes, completely free. All processing happens in your browser using AI models, so we have no server costs and your photos never leave your device.
faq.imageIdPhoto2: What photo should I upload for best results?
faq.imageIdPhoto2a: Use a photo with a plain background, good lighting, and face the camera directly. Avoid heavy shadows, sunglasses, or hats for the cleanest ID photo output.
faq.imageIdPhoto3: Are my photos uploaded to a server?
faq.imageIdPhoto3a: No. Your photos are processed entirely in your browser using AI models. Nothing is uploaded, so your portrait stays fully private.
faq.imageOcr1a: Chinese (Simplified), English, and Japanese. You can enable multiple languages at once for mixed-language images.
faq.imageOcr2a: Accuracy depends on image quality and text clarity. Clear, high-resolution images with good contrast give the best results, while blurry or skewed text may be less accurate.
faq.imageOcr3: Is my image uploaded for OCR processing?
faq.imageOcr3a: No, OCR runs entirely in your browser using Tesseract.js. Your images and the extracted text never leave your device.
faq.imageBlur1a: No, this tool uses manual selection for maximum privacy and reliability. You draw a box over each area you want to blur, giving you full control.
faq.imageBlur2a: Yes, you can blur multiple regions by selecting and applying blur one by one. Each blur is applied permanently to the image before the next.
faq.imageBlur3: What blur intensity should I use?
faq.imageBlur3a: Light for subtle blurring, Medium for most cases, and Heavy for complete obscuration of faces or license plates.
faq.imageGridSplit1: Do I need to upload my image to a server?
faq.imageGridSplit1a: No. All splitting happens in your browser. Your image never leaves your device, so it's safe for personal photos.
faq.imageGridSplit2a: Built-in presets for 2×2, 3×3, and 4×4. You can also choose Custom and set any rows × columns between 1 and 6.
faq.imageGridSplit3: Can I use it for an Instagram nine-grid post?
faq.imageGridSplit3a: Yes. Choose the 3×3 layout, split your image, and the 9 pieces are downloaded as a ZIP — ready to post in order.
faq.qrCode1: Do I need to upload anything or be online?
faq.qrCode1a: No. QR codes are generated entirely in your browser. No upload, no internet connection required after the page loads.
faq.qrCode2a: Plain text, URLs, email addresses, WiFi credentials (SSID + password + encryption), and vCard contact info (name, phone, email).
faq.qrCode3: Can I customize the QR code colors?
faq.qrCode3a: Yes. You can pick any foreground and background color, choose the size, and select the error correction level (L/M/Q/H).
faq.chartGenerator1: Do I need to upload my data?
faq.chartGenerator1a: No. All chart rendering happens locally in your browser. Your data never leaves your device.
faq.chartGenerator2a: Bar, line, and pie charts. You can add multiple series for bar and line charts (pie uses the first series only).
faq.chartGenerator3a: PNG (raster) and SVG (vector). SVG scales to any size without quality loss, ideal for print and presentations.
faq.fileTransfer1: Is my file safe during transfer?
faq.fileTransfer1a: Yes. Files transfer directly between two devices via WebRTC DataChannel. No server stores or relays file content.
faq.fileTransfer2: Why does it need a signaling server?
faq.fileTransfer2a: WebRTC requires a signaling channel to exchange connection info (SDP offer/answer). We use the public PeerJS broker which only relays this setup data, never the file itself.
faq.fileTransfer3: Does it work across different networks (not just LAN)?
faq.fileTransfer3a: Yes. As long as both devices have internet access, WebRTC can establish direct connection via STUN. In some restrictive networks, a TURN relay may be required, but file content remains end-to-end encrypted.
faq.fileTransfer4: Is there a file size limit?
faq.fileTransfer4a: Maximum 500MB per file. Very large files may be slow on poor connections.
faq.fileTransfer5: What happens if connection drops mid-transfer?
faq.fileTransfer5a: Transfer will abort and partial data is discarded. You can reconnect and try again.
faq.fileTransfer6: Do both devices need to be on Neetpix?
faq.fileTransfer6a: Yes. Both devices need to open this page (neetpix.com/tools/file-transfer) and one creates a room while the other joins with the code or QR.
faq.qrDecode1: Does QR Code Decoder upload my image?
faq.qrDecode1a: No. Decoding runs entirely in your browser using JavaScript. Your image never leaves your device.
faq.qrDecode2a: URL, WiFi, vCard (contact), email, phone, and plain text. The detected type is shown as a badge.
faq.qrDecode3: Can I scan a QR code from a screenshot?
faq.qrDecode3a: Yes. You can drag-and-drop, click to browse, or paste (Ctrl+V) a screenshot containing a QR code.
faq.qrDecode4: What if no QR code is detected?
faq.qrDecode4a: Try a clearer or higher-contrast image. Blurry, rotated, or partially obscured QR codes may fail to decode.
faq.compressPdfTo100kb1: Is it really possible to compress any PDF to under 100KB?
faq.compressPdfTo100kb1a: Most text-based PDFs can be reduced to under 100KB using Strong mode. Scanned or image-heavy PDFs may not reach 100KB without visible quality loss, but the tool will achieve the smallest possible size.
faq.compressPdfTo100kb2a: Yes, completely free with no signup, no watermark, and no upload. All processing happens in your browser.
faq.compressPdfTo100kb3: Are my files uploaded to a server?
faq.compressPdfTo100kb3a: No. Your PDF is processed entirely in your browser using WebAssembly. It never leaves your device.
faq.compressPdfTo100kb4: What's the difference between Light and Strong mode?
faq.compressPdfTo100kb4a: Light mode uses lossless structural compression (5-20% smaller, text stays searchable). Strong mode rasterizes each page as JPEG for maximum size reduction — best for reaching aggressive targets like 100KB.
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress1: Can I merge and compress PDFs in one step?
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress1a: Currently, merge and compress are separate tools. First merge your PDFs, then run the result through PDF Compress. Both run locally in your browser.
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress2: Is there a limit on the number of PDFs I can merge?
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress2a: There's no hard limit, but we recommend under 20 files for best performance.
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress3a: No. Merging preserves the original pages exactly. Use the compress tool separately if you need a smaller file.
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress4: Are my files uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfMergeAndCompress4a: No. Both merge and compress run entirely in your browser. Your PDFs never leave your device.
faq.imageToPdfConverter1a: JPG, PNG, and WebP up to 20MB each.
faq.imageToPdfConverter2: Can I reorder images after uploading?
faq.imageToPdfConverter2a: Yes, drag and drop any thumbnail to reorder.
faq.imageToPdfConverter3: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageToPdfConverter3a: No. All conversion happens in your browser. Your images never leave your device.
faq.imageToPdfConverter4a: A4, Letter, or fit to original image size. Portrait or landscape orientation.
faq.removeBackgroundFree1a: Yes, completely free. AI runs in your browser, so we have no server costs. The free version includes a small Neetpix watermark.
faq.removeBackgroundFree2a: JPG, PNG, and WebP up to 20MB.
faq.removeBackgroundFree3: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.removeBackgroundFree3a: No. All AI processing happens in your browser using ONNX models. Your images never leave your device.
faq.removeBackgroundFree4a: The result is a transparent PNG, ready to overlay on any background.
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree1: Is the QR code generator free?
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree1a: Yes, completely free with no signup and no watermark. All generation happens in your browser.
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree2a: Plain text, URLs, email addresses, WiFi credentials (SSID + password + encryption), and vCard contact info (name, phone, email).
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree3: Are my QR codes uploaded to a server?
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree3a: No. QR codes are generated entirely in your browser. No internet connection required after the page loads.
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree4: Can I customize the QR code colors?
faq.qrCodeGeneratorFree4a: Yes. You can pick any foreground and background color, choose the size, and select the error correction level (L/M/Q/H).
faq.pdfCompressOnline1: Is the online PDF compressor free?
faq.pdfCompressOnline1a: Yes, completely free with no signup, no watermark, and no upload. All processing happens in your browser.
faq.pdfCompressOnline2: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfCompressOnline2a: No. Your PDF is processed entirely in your browser using WebAssembly. It never leaves your device.
faq.pdfCompressOnline3a: Light mode uses lossless structural compression (5-20% smaller, text stays searchable). Strong mode rasterizes each page as JPEG for maximum size reduction.
faq.pdfCompressOnline4a: Up to 50MB per file. Processing happens in your browser, so very large files may be slow.
faq.wordToPdfFree1: Is the Word to PDF converter free?
faq.wordToPdfFree1a: Yes, completely free with no limits on file count or size (up to 50MB per file). No signup, no watermark, no hidden fees.
faq.wordToPdfFree2: Are my documents uploaded to a server?
faq.wordToPdfFree2a: No. All processing happens in your browser. Your documents never leave your device, ensuring complete privacy.
faq.wordToPdfFree3a: We preserve text content with basic formatting including headings, paragraphs, and lists. Complex layouts, images, and tables may be simplified.
faq.wordToPdfFree4a: Only .docx files are supported. Legacy .doc format is not supported — please save your document as .docx first.
faq.imageCompressTo50kb1: Is it really possible to compress any image to under 50KB?
faq.imageCompressTo50kb1a: Most images can reach 50KB by adjusting quality (e.g., 60-70%) or resizing. Very large or detailed images may need more aggressive compression, which can introduce visible artifacts.
faq.imageCompressTo50kb2a: Yes, completely free with no signup and no upload. All processing happens in your browser.
faq.imageCompressTo50kb3: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageCompressTo50kb3a: No. Your images are processed entirely in your browser and never leave your device.
faq.imageCompressTo50kb4: Can I convert format during compression?
faq.imageCompressTo50kb4a: Yes, you can output as JPG, PNG, or WebP regardless of the original format. WebP typically gives the smallest size at equivalent quality.
faq.removeBackground4: Are my photos uploaded to a server?
faq.removeBackground4a: No. All AI background removal runs locally in your browser using ONNX models. Your photos never leave your device — perfect for sensitive portraits and ID photos.
faq.imageToPdf4: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageToPdf4a: No. All image-to-PDF conversion happens in your browser. Your images stay on your device, ensuring complete privacy.
faq.pdfToWord4: What should I do if my PDF is scanned?
faq.pdfToWord4a: Only text-based PDFs are supported. For scanned PDFs, please use our OCR tool first.
faq.wordToPdf4a: Only .docx files are supported. Legacy .doc format is not supported — please save your document as .docx first.
faq.screenshotTranslate4: Are my screenshots uploaded to a server?
faq.screenshotTranslate4a: OCR runs in your browser, so your screenshot stays on your device. However, the extracted text is sent to the MyMemory API for translation — avoid pasting highly sensitive content.
faq.imageCompress4: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageCompress4a: No. All compression happens in your browser. Your images never leave your device — safe for sensitive photos and ID scans.
faq.pdfMerge4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfMerge4a: No. All merging happens in your browser using pdf-lib. Your PDFs never leave your device — safe for contracts and confidential documents.
faq.pdfSplit4: Is PDF split free? Do I need to sign up?
faq.pdfSplit4a: Yes, completely free with no signup, no watermark, and no upload. All splitting happens in your browser.
faq.pdfCompress4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server? Is there a size limit?
faq.pdfCompress4a: No. PDF compression runs entirely in your browser using WebAssembly. The maximum file size is 50MB per file; very large files may be slow.
faq.pdfWatermark4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfWatermark4a: No. All watermarking happens in your browser. Your PDFs never leave your device — safe for confidential contracts and legal documents.
faq.pdfPageNumbers4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfPageNumbers4a: No. Page number rendering happens entirely in your browser using pdf-lib. Your PDFs stay on your device.
faq.imageWatermark4: What image formats are supported? Can I batch process?
faq.imageWatermark4a: JPG, PNG, and WebP up to 20MB each. Batch processing is not yet supported — watermark each image one at a time.
faq.pdfEncrypt4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfEncrypt4a: No. All encryption happens in your browser using AES-256. Your PDFs never leave your device — ideal for legal, financial, and medical documents.
faq.pdfDecrypt4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfDecrypt4a: No. Decryption runs entirely in your browser. Your PDF and password never leave your device.
faq.pdfCrop4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfCrop4a: No. All cropping happens in your browser using pdf-lib. Your PDFs stay on your device — safe for confidential documents.
faq.pdfRotate4: Are my PDFs uploaded to a server?
faq.pdfRotate4a: No. Rotation only updates page orientation metadata in your browser. Your PDFs never leave your device.
faq.imageConvert4: Do I need to sign up or install software?
faq.imageConvert4a: No signup, no installation. Open the page in any modern browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge) and convert HEIC, JPG, PNG, WebP, and AVIF instantly.
faq.imageExif4a: JPG, PNG, WebP, and HEIC. EXIF data is read and stripped locally in your browser, so your photos never leave your device.
faq.imageResize4: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageResize4a: No. All resizing happens in your browser using Lanczos resampling. Your images stay on your device — safe for personal photos.
faq.imageIdPhoto4: What photo sizes and output formats are supported?
faq.imageIdPhoto4a: Common ID sizes including 1-inch, 2-inch, passport, and visa photo dimensions. Output is JPG, ready for online submission or printing.
faq.imageOcr4: Is OCR free? Can I batch process?
faq.imageOcr4a: Yes, completely free with no signup. Currently one image at a time — batch OCR is on our roadmap.
faq.imageBlur4: Are my images uploaded to a server?
faq.imageBlur4a: No. All blurring happens in your browser. Your images never leave your device — safe for photos with faces, license plates, and sensitive info.
faq.imageGridSplit4: Is image grid split free? Do I need to sign up?
faq.imageGridSplit4a: Yes, completely free with no signup and no watermark. All splitting happens in your browser.
faq.qrCode4: Is the QR code generator free? Do I need to sign up?
faq.qrCode4a: Yes, completely free with no signup, no watermark, and no expiration. Generated QR codes work forever.
faq.chartGenerator4: Is the chart generator free? Do I need to sign up?
faq.chartGenerator4a: Yes, completely free with no signup. All chart rendering happens in your browser — your data never leaves your device.

pdfMerge.title: Common Use Cases
pdfMerge.scenario1Desc: Merge lecture notes, slides, and handouts into one PDF for easier exam review. Stop juggling 10 files — keep everything in a single searchable document.
pdfMerge.scenario2Desc: Combine contracts, appendices, and cover letters into one polished PDF before sending to clients. Reorder pages freely so the final reads as a coherent package.
pdfMerge.scenario3Desc: Bundle product catalogs, spec sheets, and warranty terms into one downloadable PDF. Fewer clicks for customers means fewer support emails and higher conversion.

pdfSplit.title: Common Use Cases
pdfSplit.scenario1Desc: Split a 200-page thesis into chapter-by-chapter PDFs for easier sharing and review. Use ranges like 1-30, 31-60 to extract each chapter in one click.
pdfSplit.scenario2Desc: Extract only the relevant pages from a long contract or report before forwarding to stakeholders. Send just what they need — no noise, no clutter.
pdfSplit.scenario3Desc: Split a combined exam PDF into per-student or per-section files. Each range becomes a separate PDF, ready to print or distribute individually.

pdfCompress.title: Common Use Cases
pdfCompress.scenario1Desc: Compress your resume PDF to fit email attachment limits (often 5-10MB). Strong mode can shrink a scanned resume to under 100KB for online form uploads.
pdfCompress.scenario2Desc: Government portals, scholarship applications, and visa forms often cap PDF size at 100KB or 1MB. Hit the target with Light or Strong mode — no upload required.
pdfCompress.scenario3Desc: Shrink large PDF reports before attaching to Gmail, Outlook, or Slack. Recipients get the same content in a fraction of the size — no quality loss in Light mode.

pdfToWord.title: Common Use Cases
pdfToWord.scenario1Desc: Convert a PDF lecture handout into Word so you can add notes, highlight, and reorganize content before exams. Much faster than retyping everything.
pdfToWord.scenario2Desc: Received a contract or proposal as PDF? Convert to .docx, make edits, and send back the revised version without re-creating the document from scratch.
pdfToWord.scenario3Desc: Extract text from PDF papers into Word for citation, paraphrasing, or literature reviews. Selectable-text PDFs work best; scanned PDFs need OCR first.

wordToPdf.title: Common Use Cases
wordToPdf.scenario1Desc: Convert your resume from Word to PDF before sending to recruiters. PDF preserves formatting across devices so your layout looks identical on every screen.
wordToPdf.scenario2Desc: Finalize contracts, proposals, and reports as PDF before sharing externally. PDF is harder to accidentally edit, making it the standard for signed documents.
wordToPdf.scenario3Desc: Submit assignments as PDF so teachers see your exact formatting — fonts, headings, and images all stay in place, regardless of device or Word version.

imageToPdf.title: Common Use Cases
imageToPdf.scenario1Desc: Convert photographed lecture whiteboards or textbook pages into a single searchable PDF. Reorder pages to match the lecture flow.
imageToPdf.scenario2Desc: Combine scanned receipts, invoices, or signed forms into one PDF for expense reports or audits. No need for paid scanner software — your phone camera is enough.
imageToPdf.scenario3Desc: Bundle a photo shoot into one PDF portfolio for client preview. Choose fit-to-image or A4 layout, and reorder shots to tell the right story.

pdfWatermark.title: Common Use Cases
pdfWatermark.scenario1Desc: Mark contracts and legal drafts as CONFIDENTIAL or DRAFT before sharing with opposing counsel. Tiled watermarks make unauthorized redistribution obvious.
pdfWatermark.scenario2Desc: Add a client logo or brand watermark to PDF mockups before review. Center placement keeps the design clean while proving ownership.
pdfWatermark.scenario3Desc: Stamp exam papers and answer keys with the teacher's name or class code. Tiled watermarks discourage photocopying and leaking before test day.

pdfPageNumbers.title: Common Use Cases
pdfPageNumbers.scenario1Desc: Add page numbers to thesis or dissertation PDFs before submission. Most universities require them, and starting from a custom number handles front matter correctly.
pdfPageNumbers.scenario2Desc: Number multi-page reports and proposals so reviewers can reference specific pages in feedback. Bottom-right is the most common professional choice.
pdfPageNumbers.scenario3Desc: Add page numbers to book manuscripts and PDF booklets. Use 'Page X / Y' format so readers always know how much is left.

pdfEncrypt.title: Common Use Cases
pdfEncrypt.scenario1Desc: Password-protect contracts, NDA agreements, and settlement documents before emailing. AES-256 encryption ensures only intended recipients can open them.
pdfEncrypt.scenario2Desc: Encrypt employee records, payroll PDFs, and benefit statements before sharing internally or externally. Avoid data breaches from leaked email attachments.
pdfEncrypt.scenario3Desc: Protect tax returns, audit reports, and financial statements with a password. Required by many compliance frameworks (GDPR, HIPAA) for sensitive data.

pdfDecrypt.title: Common Use Cases
pdfDecrypt.scenario1Desc: Remove passwords from your own PDFs when you no longer need protection — for example, after a contract is signed and stored in your archive.
pdfDecrypt.scenario2Desc: Decrypt purchased e-books or papers (that you own) for easier reading across devices. Note: only works if you know the password.
pdfDecrypt.scenario3Desc: Batch-remove passwords from internal documents when migrating to a new document management system that enforces its own access controls.

pdfCrop.title: Common Use Cases
pdfCrop.scenario1Desc: Remove black edges from scanned pages caused by scanner glass boundaries. Crop a uniform percentage from each side for a clean, professional look.
pdfCrop.scenario2Desc: Trim excess white margins from PDF mockups or posters before client review. Focus attention on the actual content.
pdfCrop.scenario3Desc: Crop a chapter from a textbook PDF to print only what you need. Saves paper, ink, and carries less weight in your bag.

pdfRotate.title: Common Use Cases
pdfRotate.scenario1Desc: Fix pages scanned sideways or upside-down. Rotate specific pages 90° or 180° without affecting the rest of the document.
pdfRotate.scenario2Desc: Correct orientation of PDFs created from phone photos. Landscape shots often end up sideways — rotate just those pages in one click.
pdfRotate.scenario3Desc: Rotate landscape pages in a PDF textbook so they're easier to read on a portrait monitor or tablet. Specific-page rotation keeps the rest untouched.

removeBackground.title: Common Use Cases
removeBackground.scenario1Desc: Remove backgrounds from product photos to get clean, white-background listings for Amazon, Shopify, and eBay. Boost click-through with consistent catalog imagery.
removeBackground.scenario2Desc: Cut yourself out of any photo and drop into new backgrounds for thumbnails, posters, or memes. No green screen or Photoshop needed.
removeBackground.scenario3Desc: Quickly isolate subjects for composites, mockups, or branding assets. Transparent PNG output drops directly into Figma, Canva, or Photoshop.

imageCompress.title: Common Use Cases
imageCompress.scenario1Desc: Shrink product images and hero banners before uploading to Shopify, WordPress, or Webflow. Smaller images mean faster page loads and higher SEO rankings.
imageCompress.scenario2Desc: Compress photos to fit Gmail's 25MB attachment limit. Convert RAW or HEIC to JPG on the fly and attach more photos per email.
imageCompress.scenario3Desc: Hit the 50KB or 100KB image limits common on government, scholarship, and visa forms. Lower the quality slider until you hit the target.

imageWatermark.title: Common Use Cases
imageWatermark.scenario1Desc: Add your logo or studio name to photos before sharing proofs online. Tiled watermarks discourage clients from using unwatermarked previews.
imageWatermark.scenario2Desc: Watermark product images to prevent competitors from copying your listing photos. Brand watermarks also reinforce your store identity.
imageWatermark.scenario3Desc: Stamp your handle or website on viral-bound images and memes. If a post gets shared hundreds of times, your watermark travels with it.

imageConvert.title: Common Use Cases
imageConvert.scenario1Desc: Convert HEIC photos to JPG for compatibility with older Windows PCs, web forms, and editing tools that don't support Apple's newer format.
imageConvert.scenario2Desc: Batch-convert PNGs to WebP for smaller file sizes on modern websites. WebP can cut image weight by 25-35% vs PNG at equivalent quality.
imageConvert.scenario3Desc: Convert RAW or AVIF shots to JPG for quick client previews. AVIF support is still uneven across editors, so JPG remains the universal deliverable.

imageExif.title: Common Use Cases
imageExif.scenario1Desc: Strip GPS and device metadata from photos before posting to Instagram, Facebook, or Twitter. Protect your home address and daily routine from strangers.
imageExif.scenario2Desc: Remove EXIF from photos sent over insecure channels to protect sources and locations. Critical for conflict reporting and whistleblower communications.
imageExif.scenario3Desc: Clean metadata from product photos before listing. Some marketplaces reject images with embedded camera info; stripping EXIF ensures smooth upload.

imageResize.title: Common Use Cases
imageResize.scenario1Desc: Resize one photo into multiple aspect ratios — Instagram square (1:1), Story (9:16), Twitter (16:9) — using built-in presets. No more manual cropping.
imageResize.scenario2Desc: Standardize product images to consistent dimensions for cleaner catalog layouts. Batch-resize hundreds of photos and download as a ZIP.
imageResize.scenario3Desc: Resize photos to specific pixel dimensions required by online forms (e.g., 600×600 for US visas, 35×45mm for Indian passports).

imageIdPhoto.title: Common Use Cases
imageIdPhoto.scenario1Desc: Generate passport and visa photos at home — no trip to a photo studio. Use a plain wall as background and AI handles the rest.
imageIdPhoto.scenario2Desc: Create ID photos for school enrollment, library cards, or exam registrations. Print at home or submit online — saves time and money vs. drugstore photo booths.
imageIdPhoto.scenario3Desc: Make professional headshots for LinkedIn, company badges, or conference badges. A clean background and good lighting are all you need.

imageOcr.title: Common Use Cases
imageOcr.scenario1Desc: Extract text from photographed textbook pages or lecture slides into searchable notes. Skip retyping quotes and citations — copy directly from the image.
imageOcr.scenario2Desc: Digitize scanned contracts, invoices, or receipts into editable text. Search and paste content without re-typing from a paper scan.
imageOcr.scenario3Desc: OCR foreign-language menus, signs, or documents while traveling. Multi-language support handles Chinese, English, and Japanese in one image.

imageBlur.title: Common Use Cases
imageBlur.scenario1Desc: Blur faces of sources, bystanders, or minors in photos before publication. Manual selection gives precise control over what's redacted.

pdf-tools-privacy-risk.title: The Hidden Privacy Risks of Online PDF Tools
pdf-tools-privacy-risk.description: Most online PDF tools upload your documents to remote servers. Learn what happens to your files and how browser-local processing keeps them private.
content: PDFs are the workhorse format for contracts, invoices, ID scans, tax forms, and medical records. When you need to merge, compress, or convert one in a hurry, a quick search surfaces dozens of free online tools. But behind the friendly drag-and-drop interface of almost every popular PDF service sits a quieter reality: your file is being uploaded to a remote server, and you have very little control over what happens next.

To understand why this matters, it helps to know how server-based PDF tools actually work. When you drop a file into a typical online PDF merger or converter, the browser sends the entire file over HTTPS to the vendor's storage backend. The server runs the actual processing — qpdf, Ghostscript, headless LibreOffice, or a proprietary pipeline — generates the result, and streams it back to your browser. The file sits on the vendor's disk for at least the duration of the job, and in many cases much longer.

What happens to your uploaded documents after processing varies widely between vendors. Some delete the file within minutes. Others retain it for hours, days, or until you delete it from your dashboard. A few explicitly state that files may be reviewed by humans for quality, security, or abuse prevention. That sentence is easy to miss in a privacy policy, but it means a stranger could open your tax return or your signed lease.

The breach problem makes this worse. Even vendors with strict deletion policies cannot guarantee your file is gone forever. Backups, log attachments, error tracking systems, and cloud storage snapshots can retain copies for months. When a vendor suffers a breach, those orphaned copies are exactly what attackers are after. The PDF format is dense with personally identifiable information — names, addresses, signatures, Social Security numbers, bank account details — which makes PDF tooling servers high-value targets.

There is no shortage of real-world incidents. Privacy-conscious users have pointed to episodes like the 2017 DocuView exposure and recurring reports of files from popular online converters resurfacing in third-party indexes. TinyPNG disclosed a 2019 breach that exposed API keys and source data. These are not abstract risks; they are the predictable outcome of building a service that asks users to hand over their documents.

The good news is that you can verify in seconds whether a tool is uploading your file. Open your browser's DevTools, switch to the Network tab, and start a conversion. If you see a POST request with a large upload to an S3 bucket, an API endpoint, or any domain other than the one you are on, your file is leaving your device. A tool that processes locally will show no such upload — the work happens inside the page's WebAssembly sandbox.

Neetpix was built on the opposite assumption. Every PDF operation — merge, split, compress, watermark, encrypt, rotate, crop, page numbers, and PDF-to-Word conversion — runs directly in your browser using WebAssembly ports of qpdf, pdf-lib, and PDF.js. The file you select never leaves your device. There is no upload endpoint, no server-side storage, and no human review pipeline. The only network traffic is the page itself.

The browser-local model has practical benefits beyond privacy. Because there is no upload or download round-trip, processing starts instantly and finishes as fast as your CPU allows. A 50-page merge that takes thirty seconds round-trip on a server-based tool typically completes in two or three seconds in Neetpix. For batch workflows where you process many files in a row, the time savings compounds.

If you are evaluating any online PDF tool, look for three signals. First, a clear 100% local processing badge backed by a technical explanation — WebAssembly, in-browser, no upload. Second, an open DevTools test that confirms no file upload occurs. Third, a privacy policy that does not include language about file retention or human review. If any of these are missing, treat the tool as a server-based service regardless of marketing.

Certain document categories deserve extra caution. Signed contracts and NDAs often contain competitive intelligence that would be valuable to a breach buyer. Medical records are regulated under HIPAA in the US and similar frameworks elsewhere, which means a single leaked PDF can trigger mandatory disclosure obligations and reputational harm. Tax returns and bank statements are the raw materials of identity theft, and a single W-2 or 1099 in the wrong hands is enough to file a fraudulent return in your name. If you would not email a document to a stranger, you should not upload it to an unverified PDF tool either.

PDFs carry some of your most sensitive information. The convenience of a free online tool is not worth a leaked contract or a stolen identity. Try our PDF Merge or PDF to Word and watch your Network tab: nothing leaves your device. Once you have experienced browser-local PDF tooling, the upload-based model starts to look like a relic.

smallpdf-free-alternative.title: The Best Free Smallpdf Alternative in 2026
smallpdf-free-alternative.description: Smallpdf limits free users to 2 tasks per day and uploads files to its servers. Neetpix is fully free, unlimited, and processes everything in your browser.
content: Smallpdf is one of the most recognizable names in online PDF tooling. Its clean interface and broad feature set have made it a go-to for quick document edits since 2013. But if you have used the free tier recently, you have probably hit the 2-tasks-per-day wall, the upload-to-server prompts, and the constant nudges toward a Pro subscription. This article breaks down exactly where Smallpdf's free tier falls short in 2026 and why Neetpix is the strongest no-compromise alternative.

Start with the daily task limit. As of 2026, Smallpdf's free plan restricts users to 2 tasks per day. Need to merge three PDFs in the morning and compress a fourth in the afternoon? You are locked out until tomorrow. The free tier also restricts access to certain tools entirely — PDF/A conversion, OCR, and e-signatures sit behind the paywall — and inserts prompts to upgrade after almost every action.

Then there is price. Smallpdf Pro is currently priced at $9 per month when billed annually, or $12 when billed monthly. A team plan runs from $10 per seat. For users who only occasionally need to merge or compress a PDF, that is a meaningful recurring cost for functionality that can run entirely in a browser tab. Over a year, a solo subscriber pays more than $100 for what is essentially a wrapper around open-source PDF libraries.

The more serious issue is the upload problem. Like most server-based PDF services, Smallpdf uploads your file for processing. Their FAQ states files are deleted after a defined window — historically two hours — but during that window your document lives on their infrastructure, exposed to the same breach and retention risks we covered in our PDF privacy risk article. For contracts, ID scans, or anything containing personally identifiable information, that is a real consideration.

Enter Neetpix. Neetpix is a free, browser-local alternative that covers the same PDF essentials as Smallpdf — merge, split, compress, convert, watermark, encrypt, rotate, crop, and page numbers — plus a broader suite of image tools (background removal, image compress, image-to-PDF) and generators (QR codes, charts). Everything runs in your browser via WebAssembly. There is no signup, no upload, and no daily task limit.

The side-by-side comparison makes the difference concrete. On price, Neetpix is $0 forever; Smallpdf is $0 with severe limits or $9 per month for Pro. On daily task limit, Neetpix has none; Smallpdf caps at 2. On file upload, Neetpix never uploads; Smallpdf uploads every file. On signup, neither requires an account for the free tier, but Smallpdf gates Pro features behind one. On tool count, Neetpix offers 25+ tools; Smallpdf offers around 20. On offline capability, Neetpix works offline once the page is loaded; Smallpdf requires connectivity to its processing servers.

Speed is where the local-processing model really pulls ahead. Because Neetpix does not upload or download files, processing starts instantly and finishes as fast as your CPU allows. A 50-page merge that takes thirty seconds round-trip on a server-based tool typically completes in two or three seconds in Neetpix. For batch workflows — compress ten PDFs in a row, convert five Word documents to PDF — the time savings compounds into minutes saved per week.

There are still places where Smallpdf makes sense. Its OCR, e-signature, and PDF/A archiving tools genuinely add value, and the paid plan is reasonable for teams that need them daily. If you are a heavy user of those specific features, Smallpdf Pro remains a defensible choice. For everyone else — students, freelancers, small business owners, anyone doing occasional PDF edits — there is no reason to pay or accept upload risks.

File size and format support also favor Neetpix for typical workflows. Smallpdf caps free uploads at 5 MB per file, which excludes many real-world PDFs. Neetpix has no such cap because the file never leaves your device — the only limit is your machine's memory. Both tools support the standard PDF, JPG, PNG, and Office formats, but Neetpix additionally handles WebP, HEIC, and a broader image pipeline.

Team collaboration features are worth a closer look too. Smallpdf's team plan adds shared workspaces, admin controls, and audit logs, which are useful for organizations that process many documents. Neetpix, being browser-local, does not have a server-side team workspace, but the trade-off is that there is nothing to share, store, or audit because files never leave individual devices. For most small teams, the simpler workflow of each person processing their own files locally is actually safer and easier to manage than a shared document pipeline.

Switching is trivial. Open Neetpix, pick the tool you need, drop in your file, and download the result. No account to create, no payment to authorize, no file to clean up afterward. Your browser tab is the entire workflow. Bookmark neetpix.com and you are done. Try our PDF Merge, PDF Compress, or PDF to Word — three of the most-used Smallpdf replacements — and see for yourself.

remove-bg-free-alternative.title: Free Remove.bg Alternative: No Upload, No Limit
remove-bg-free-alternative.description: Remove.bg charges for HD downloads and uploads images to its servers. Neetpix removes backgrounds locally in your browser with transformers.js + onnxruntime-web.
content: Remove.bg is the household name in AI background removal. Type remove background into Google and it is the first result almost every time. The output quality is genuinely impressive — clean cutouts, soft edges, and respectable handling of hair and translucent objects. But behind that polished result sits a payment wall and an upload pipeline that privacy-conscious users should think twice about.

Start with the price. Remove.bg's free tier downloads images at a preview resolution of roughly 0.25 megapixels — about 612 × 408 pixels. To download the full-resolution result, you pay per image or subscribe. Subscriptions start around $9.99 per month for 100 credits, with larger plans scaling up to enterprise tiers. For anyone working with product photography, social media assets, or design mockups, those credits disappear quickly. A single busy afternoon of editing can burn through a month's allocation.

The upload problem is the more fundamental issue. When you upload an image to Remove.bg, it is sent to Kaleido AI's servers in Austria for inference. The company's privacy policy is clear that images are processed on their infrastructure and retained for a period to improve model quality. For users handling ID photos, medical images, internal product mockups, or children's photos, that is a non-trivial exposure. Once an image is on a third-party server, you depend on that vendor's deletion practices, breach posture, and subcontractors.

The free-resolution cap is also a quiet productivity tax. A 0.25-megapixel preview is fine for a quick mockup but useless for anything that will be printed, displayed at full size, or composited into a larger design. The result is that free users either pay per image, subscribe, or stitch together workarounds like upscaling the preview with a separate tool — none of which is actually free. The friction pushes users toward the paid plan by design.

Neetpix takes a different approach. Our Remove Background tool runs the same class of AI segmentation model — based on MODNet and U2Net architectures — directly in your browser using transformers.js and onnxruntime-web. The model weights download once, cache aggressively, and execute against your device's GPU or CPU. The image you select never leaves your browser tab. There is no upload endpoint and no server-side storage.

The local-processing model dissolves both problems at once. There is no per-image cost because there is no server to bill against. There is no upload exposure because there is no upload. And there is no resolution cap because the inference runs against whatever image you give it — 4K, 8K, panoramic, whatever your machine can hold in memory. The output PNG is generated locally and saved directly to your downloads folder.

The trade-off is that browser-local inference is constrained by your device. A low-end laptop will take longer to process a 12-megapixel image than a high-end desktop. The free version of Neetpix's background remover also includes a small corner watermark — a fair trade for unlimited HD output with no upload. For most users and most workflows, the speed difference is barely noticeable and the watermark is easy to crop out for non-critical use.

Output quality compares well. Side by side, Neetpix's local model produces cutouts that are visually indistinguishable from Remove.bg's output on most photographs and product shots. Hair edges, translucent fabric, and complex shadows are handled with similar finesse. Where Remove.bg pulls ahead is in the hardest cases — fine blonde hair against a busy background, for instance — but the gap has narrowed significantly with recent model updates and improved post-processing.

Common use cases benefit particularly from the local model. E-commerce sellers processing dozens of product photos a week can run the entire batch through Neetpix without worrying about credit depletion. Social media managers can iterate quickly on thumbnails without upload latency. Designers can drop in client work without worrying about confidentiality agreements. Students and hobbyists get professional-grade results without a credit card.

For privacy-sensitive workflows, the comparison is not even close. ID photos, medical images, internal product mockups, children's photos — none of these should be uploaded to a third-party inference service. Running inference locally means the image stays on your device, in your control, and never appears in a vendor's training corpus or breach disclosure. In regulated industries like healthcare and finance, this is not just a preference; it is a compliance requirement.

A note on the model download size. The first time you use Neetpix's Remove Background, the browser downloads the segmentation model — typically under 10 MB. Subsequent uses load from cache, so there is no repeated download. On a typical home broadband connection, the initial download finishes in a few seconds and never happens again. This is a fundamentally different cost model from per-image API pricing, where every single removal incurs a charge against your account.

Trying it out takes about thirty seconds. Open Neetpix's Remove Background, drop in an image, and watch the result render in your browser. Open DevTools' Network tab and confirm there is no upload. The same tab handles the model download, the inference, and the output compositing. If you have been paying Remove.bg for occasional background removal, you will not go back.

tinypng-free-alternative.title: TinyPNG Free Alternative: Compress Images Locally
tinypng-free-alternative.description: TinyPNG caps free users at 20 images per month and uploads them to its servers. Neetpix compresses images in your browser with no limit and no upload.
content: TinyPNG is the first tool most designers reach for when they need to shrink a PNG or JPEG. The compression quality is excellent — often 60 to 80 percent file-size reduction with no visible loss — and the drag-and-drop interface is genuinely delightful. But like most popular online tools, TinyPNG's free tier carries quiet limits and an upload pipeline that deserves a second look.

The free tier limit is the most visible pain point. As of 2026, TinyPNG's web interface caps free users at 20 images per month. That is 20 total — not 20 per session, not 20 per day. If you process a batch of 15 product photos in early January, you have 5 compressions left for the rest of the month. For anyone who works with images regularly, the cap bites fast.

The paid plan lifts the limit but introduces a recurring cost. TinyPNG Pro is currently priced at around $25 per year for individuals, with API pricing for developers that charges per compression. The API is generous — 500 free compressions per month for developers — but the web interface, which is what most casual users touch, stays metered. The result is a tool that quietly pushes occasional users toward a subscription.

The more fundamental concern is upload. When you drop an image into TinyPNG's web interface, it is uploaded to their servers for processing. Their FAQ states that images are deleted within a short window — historically around 20 minutes — but during that window your image sits on their infrastructure. For sensitive images (ID scans, internal product shots, medical photography), even a brief upload window is a real consideration.

TinyPNG's API also had a notable breach in 2019. The company disclosed that API keys and source data were exposed for a subset of users. The breach was contained and the company responded transparently, but it underscores a general truth: any service that asks you to upload images is one breach away from exposing them. Browser-local processing sidesteps the entire category of risk.

Neetpix offers a TinyPNG alternative that runs entirely in your browser. Our Image Compress tool uses the browser-image-compression library combined with native Canvas and WebCodecs APIs to shrink JPEG, PNG, and WebP files. The image you select never leaves your device. There is no signup, no upload, and no monthly cap.

The compression results compare well. For JPEGs, Neetpix typically achieves 60 to 80 percent reduction at visually lossless quality — matching TinyPNG's output. For PNGs, the lossless mode preserves alpha channels and indexed colors; a lossy mode uses quantization to push sizes down further. WebP output is supported alongside JPEG and PNG, which is useful for modern web workflows.

The local-processing model has practical advantages beyond privacy. Because there is no upload, batch compression starts instantly and processes images in parallel across CPU cores. A folder of 50 product photos that takes several minutes to upload, process, and download through TinyPNG typically finishes in under a minute on Neetpix. There is no queue position, no rate limit, no waiting for the server to free up capacity.

Format flexibility is another area where Neetpix pulls ahead. TinyPNG supports JPEG, PNG, and WebP inputs but outputs in the original format. Neetpix lets you convert during compression — for instance, compressing a PNG and outputting as WebP for a smaller file at equivalent quality. This matters for web performance budgets where every kilobyte counts, and where WebP is now universally supported in modern browsers.

For batch workflows, Neetpix is significantly smoother. Drag in 30 images at once, set a target size or quality, and the tool processes them in parallel with a per-image progress indicator. Download them individually or as a zip. No per-image upload delay, no you have used 15 of 20 free compressions this month nag, no upgrade prompts between batches.

TinyPNG still has its place. The Photoshop and Figma plugins are excellent for designers who live in those tools, and the API is well-documented for developers building compression into their own pipelines. If you are a heavy plugin user, TinyPNG Pro is a defensible choice. For everyone else doing web image compression, Neetpix matches the quality, removes the cap, and keeps your images on your device.

A note on transparency. Neetpix's compression runs in the open: the browser-image-compression library is open source, and you can inspect the source of the page you are on. TinyPNG's compression algorithm, by contrast, is proprietary and runs on their servers, which means you cannot verify what happens to your image between upload and download. For most users this does not matter, but for privacy-sensitive workflows it is the difference between trusting a vendor and verifying a tool.

Trying it takes thirty seconds. Open Neetpix's Image Compress, drop in a few images, set a target size or quality, and download the results. Open DevTools' Network tab and confirm there is no upload. The same tab handles the entire pipeline. Once you have experienced unlimited local compression, the 20-per-month cap feels like an artifact from an earlier era of the web.
